data.title: Progressive: No Longer A Premium On This Insurer
data.description:
At the end of September of last year, I labeled Progressive (NYSE:PGR) a 'buy' in my article on this insurer. And Progressive definitely delivered operationally so far; however, I also mentioned that the valuations at the time were somewhat stretched. The market agreed, and even though the company performed really well, the current share price is lower than in September. This multiple compression has further improved Progressive, making it a strong buy at current levels.

Performance overview

Looking at the financial performance over the period since my previous rating, it is clear that there is a big underperformance as compared to the S&P 500:

To be clear, less than a year is a very limited window to judge performance, but the gap to a broad benchmark such as the S&P 500 is significant. Short-term shareholders are most likely not very happy with such a run. Do note the roughly 2% difference between price return and total return is due to the 10 cents quarterly dividend and the special distribution of USD 4.50 earlier this year.

I already mentioned that the return development can largely be ascribed to compression of multiples for the business. The table below is proof of it.

Previously the company had a TTM P/E of 22.4, and now it stands at a more reasonable ~ 14. This is far below the 5Y average for Progressive, so by this metric along, the current share price is far more appealing. Without covering each single metric listed, the overall picture is that on practically every valuation metric the business is now cheaper than it was last year. And also largely cheaper than Progressive traded on average over the last five years.

While the share price contracted, the underlying business gave no reason for this contraction. If we compare the amount of policies in force at the end of August 2024 compared to the end of June 2025, we can see that this has grown by over 10%, to a total number of more than 37.3 million active policies.

For a mature company such as Progressive, this is an impressive amount of growth in less than a year.

Growth in the number of policies is not the only great growth number. Looking at the table below, many growth metrics are still far beyond the sector median.

In many cases the difference is several hundred percent better. And again often better than the 5Y average of Progressive itself. EPS figures in this regard are a bit more wonky, since this is significantly impacted by movements of the financial markets. These movements can cause accounting losses or gains of the sizable investment portfolio of Progressive, which largely consists of bonds. Taking this into account, the overall growth profile does look excellent.

Profitability is another area that displays great strengths. In my previous article I wrote the following:

'In this sector, Return on Equity is one of my preferred yardsticks to evaluate performance and with a 5-year average of over 22%, Progressive performance far exceeds the sector median of 10.33%. When looking at Return on Assets, the comparison becomes even better. Lastly, seeing how little capex is needed from total revenue to run the business, is great. In general, you want businesses that require little capex to operate, but can still absorb large amounts of capital, which can be used without diminished rates of return. Progressive is such a business and this is one of the prime reasons why the performance over the decades has been so good as it is.'

I still stand by these words, and it is actually quite amazing to see that a decades old car insurance business of this scale is able to keep on delivering and improving. Return on Equity now even stands at over 37%. You would expect such numbers for a high-flying tech company, but not for Progressive. Admittedly, Progressive of course uses its fair share of refined technology to achieve these numbers, but still, it is remarkable.

Having looked at both the performance of Progressive, the share and of Progressive, the business, it to me now seems that it is an exceptionally well-run insurance company, and has been for decades and now trades as an ordinary company that does not have such a track record.

Upcoming earnings

Next week Progressive will release its full earnings report for Q2 and its investor relations department will host an event to amongst other things discuss the pricing mechanics. While all details of the Q2 performance will only be known next week, much is already known about it since Progressive very handily publishes monthly reports. So let's inspect what is available:

Diluted comprehensive income for H1 2025 clocks in at USD 12.03 per share versus USD 6.25 in the same period last year. This is an actual improvement of over 90%. Again, not bad at all. This increase can be attributed to several factors. One is the increase in policies in force, as previously mentioned.

The second factor stems from the strong combined ratio, which stands at 86.2 for Q2 2025 as compared to 91.9 for Q2 2024.

For those who are not familiar with the term combined ratio, it states in essence how profitable an insurance company is on its underwriting operations. The number translates to the amount of costs incurred for net premiums written. If the number is above hundred, an insurance loses money on its underwriting operations. In general it can be said that lower is better.

The final factor that helped improve the comprehensive income for the period is the increase of pretax annualized investment income book yield. Which rose to 4.2% from 3.8% at the end of H1 2024. As said, Progressive has like many insurance companies a sizable portfolio of investments and this is now also yielding higher. With the Fed not yet lowering rates, I expect this area of the business to keep performing in similar fashion for the foreseeable future. This combination of growing sales, favourable combined ratios, and a higher yielding portfolio is an excellent engine to drive future growth.

Risks

Previously I mentioned multiple contraction as a risk factor, and that seems to have been right in hindsight so far. While I deem the current multiples very attractive, this does not mean that this could go down further. After all, Progressive is still trading at a premium to many other insurance companies. The risk regarding loosening underwriting standards also something that needs to be monitored. Given the fine results in this regard it does not seem to be an issue so far, but once the combined ratio for a longer period starts creeping up, it does become a red flag. Other side of this coin is that competitors could be willing to underwrite insurances at a poorer standard. This for example to seize market share. While this would directly impact Progressive's combined ratio, it could hurt its sales growth.

Conclusion

Previously I called Progressive a 'buy' and so far that has been a mixed bag. Operationally this was absolutely correct. But also my risk warning of multiple contraction seems to have been the right call so far. The result is that as compared with September, that Progressive as an investment has seriously underperformed. However, I am now even stronger convinced by Progressive as an investment. Not only is it now more likely that operational performance will deliver satisfying results, no, now it is also a less risky proposition due to the more reasonable multiple to be paid per share. This does not just reduce downside risk, but offers potential multiple expansion if Progressive starts trading inline with its 5Y average multiples. All these elements combined make Progressive a 'strong buy'.

data.title: AIA: Not The Best Time To Make Concentrated Bets On Larger Asian Markets
data.description:
Time to Reconsider Dominant Large Caps in Major Asian Markets

Asian equities have substantially outperformed emerging markets in the long run, and many markets appear better positioned in terms of valuation and growth potential.

The iShares Asia 50 ETF (NASDAQ:AIA) has outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) by over 40 percentage points in the past decade.

As an investment vehicle, this ETF has been very effective at tracking its benchmark index, which consists of the top 50 largest equities in Asia. It has also delivered stable long-term returns.

While Asian equities have outperformed substantially, there are still many issues that make investing in some of the larger markets riskier for H2 2025.

  • ETFs like this have very concentrated exposure to China and Taiwan, a move that carries more risk based on political events in recent weeks.
  • Dominant Asian markets trade at a premium to MSCI Emerging Markets, after having a strong run in H1 2025. Meanwhile, emerging Asian markets trade at much more compelling valuation.
  • Asian equities have performed very well so far this year, despite trade war and macro concerns, but this has left valuations stretched in many of these markets
  • Investing in these markets also leaves you heavily exposed to IT and financial stocks, with minimal exposure to other favorable themes like consumer staples.

Based on recent movements in the equity markets and macro/political developments, it makes sense to be underweight China and Taiwan, especially since these markets make up nearly half of the MSCI EM Index and nearly three fourths of this ETF.

This article will cover some of the macro developments in these markets, coupled with favorable equity market movements in H1 2025, and explore some of the more undiscovered Asian markets.

Overview

There are several important characteristics to note about this ETF, which make it better to seek other investment vehicles.

The management fee of 0.5% is relatively high, especially since it merely tracks dominant larger caps in Asian markets, but still slightly lower than that of some peer ETFs like the MSCI Emerging Markets ETF and the Global X FTSE Southeast Asia ETF (ASEA).

Although it invests in over 50 listed companies, it is strongly dominated by its top 10 holdings. Moreover, this ETF trades at a slight premium to MSCI EMs, and there are plenty of undervalued Asian alternatives to consider.

The index this ETF tracks invests in 50 of the largest companies in Asia, found in markets like China, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore, and South Korea. The average market cap is between $2.7-$941 billion, and the median market cap is $36.3 billion.

The first and most obvious issue to consider is the dominance of the top companies in this ETF's weighting. Taiwan Semiconductor accounts for 23% of this ETF's assets, and the top 5 companies account for nearly half of this ETF's assets.

While it invests in a variety of other smaller companies, most accounting for less than 3% of the ETF's assets, these names are all heavily concentrated in tech and banking stocks in China and Taiwan. Around 74% of its assets are invested in China and Taiwan, and its IT and financial holdings account for around 64% of its total assets.

Dominant themes like energy, real estate, healthcare, and industrials only account for less than 4% of its total assets, while markets like Hong Kong and Singapore are not significant contributors to its performance.

Growth Outlook with a Focus on China

The main concern on many investors' minds has been Q2 2025 growth, yet this outlook appears very favorable in many markets. The US economy shocked economists by delivering 3% YoY growth last quarter, easily dodging a recession.

Many economies in Asia have also had strong economic performance.

China's Economy Largely Undisturbed: China's economy also has a relatively favorable growth outlook, despite trade war tensions. China's economy grew by over 5% during the first half of this year, driven by rising industrial output, exports, and increased investments. Retail sales in China also rose by 5%.

One important fact to note is that China's export growth has been very healthy, as it has been able to diversify away from the United States to other partners.

China's manufacturing PMI rose to over 50 last month, a substantial recovery from May.

Taiwan Economy Outlook: Latest estimates for Taiwan show that the country's economic growth in Q2 2025 likely topped growth in Q1 2025.

Taiwan's export growth has been very healthy during the first half of 2025, despite concerns over the trade war. The greater risk in Taiwan lies in the general cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry, as seen in years like 2023.

South Korea Economy: South Korea has been a slight area of concern, as political risks have soared and concerns over electronics export-oriented growth have also risen. Korea's Central Bank projects that growth will fall below 1% this year, driven by weaker exports and slower consumption growth. South Korean equities have slowly begun to catch up with some Asian peers, after rebounding substantially after Q1 2025.

Asia Outperforms Global Equities: 2025 has been a healthy year for Asian equities so far. Asia Ex. Japan markets have continued to outperform in 2025, ahead of the S&P 500 and European markets.

China has been one of the main drivers of this solid performance in Asian markets.

As we have seen during previous trade war tensions during the previous Trump administration, major Asian equity markets still have the potential to perform well amid trade war tensions. However, a 20+% run in markets like China seems a bit overdone, and these markets could be due for a pullback on the back of any unexpected economic news.

Looking Elsewhere

The lack of geographical and industry diversification seems to be a common issue among Asia-focused ETFs. The Global X FTSE Southeast Asia also invests over 60% of its assets in 2 markets, and invests around 60% of its assets in financials.

Equity markets like the Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand trade at around 11-14x earnings, a significant discount to this ETF.

These markets have been shielded from some of the dominant Asia large-cap market gains, and have consequently underperformed this ETF by 24-40 percentage points. However, these ETFs are much more undervalued at the moment and have a much more diversified structure.

ETF Top 3 Industries Data ( % of total assets) Top 10 Holdings % of Total Assets
iShares MSCI Thailand (THD)

Industrials: 23.3%

Energy: 16.0%

Financials: 11.5%

55.5%
VanEck Indonesia ETF (IDX)

Financials: 24.9%

Basic Materials: 23.7%

Energy: 11.5%

56.8%
iShares MSCI Philippines ETF (EPHE)

Financials: 23.2%

Industrials: 22.8%

Consumer Cyclical: 17.8%

64.6%
iShares MSCI Malaysia ETF (EWM)

Financials: 43.8%

Industrials: 11.9%

Utilities: 11.5%

63.9%

These markets all have a much more diversified industry makeup and also trade at a substantial discount to other larger Asian stock markets. There are several key factors to note:

  • Political risks in Thailand have picked up in recent months, and this may not be the best market to hold in the coming months.
  • The Vaneck Indonesia ETF is a smaller ETF, but intriguing because it is not overly concentrated in financials, like its peer ETF, the iShares MSCI Indonesia ETF.
  • These economies, with the exception of Thailand, should grow by around 4-6% this year.
  • The top 10 holdings still account for over half of these exchange traded funds' assets.

The best approach to investing in Asia would be to invest in a basket of country ETFs, with a 5-20% weighting assigned to each one. These emerging markets, and even dominant markets like Singapore and Hong Kong, do not even account for 5% of the assets of many Asian ETFs. Furthermore, smaller, undervalued emerging markets like Indonesia, the Philippines, and Malaysia could be positioned to outperform China in the coming quarters.

data.title: TWOD: Baby Bond From Two Harbors Currently Overpriced
data.description:
Thesis

Two Harbors Investment Corp. CAL 30 (NYSE:TWOD) is a baby bond from the mortgage Reit ('mReit') Two Harbors (TWO). In today's article we are going to discuss the mReit's most recent financials as well as TWOD's structure and analytics, and highlight for investors why we believe the security is currently overpriced.

Q2 Results

The mReit just reported its latest financials, and the results are less than stellar:

The company recorded a large loss per share for the quarter, with a negative economic return on book value. Its leverage is still very high at 7x for its portfolio, which means negative returns are amplified.

While we are not investors in the common shares, it is important for a debt investor in the company to see a stable book value, or a slightly decreasing one. The trend here, however, is worrisome:

The book value had a massive hit, decreasing from $14.6 per share to $12.14 per share. The main driver for the book value decrease is a legacy lawsuit which has now been provisioned for:

On May 23, the court ruled that Two Harbors did not have grants to terminate its management agreement for costs. And so we, in consultation with our independent accountants and legal advisers, determined that the loss was not probable and estimable under ASC 450, which is the accounting standard that governs loss contingencies. The amount of the loss contingency accrual is the same $140 million that we initially reserved in 2020 related to the nonrenewal of the management agreement before reversing the accrual in connection with the subsequent termination for costs. The current accrual also includes an assumed statutory prejudgment interest at the simple rate of 9%. No other potential losses are probable or estimable at this time. We are waiting for a trial date to be set to resolve certain claims related to intellectual property and on the issues of potential damages for the contract termination.

The company stated that excluding the lawsuit accrual, the total quarterly economic return would have been -1.4%, a more palatable figure. While one-time losses are not necessarily worrisome when the underlying business is robust, it is not the case here. TWO is an mReit, not a company that makes widgets, thus any cash leaving the company is ultimately bad for bondholders.

Even without the reserve for the lawsuit contingency, the company would have lost money:

Please note the company lost $100 mm from its portfolio of agency bonds, MSR and hedges, versus a flat performance in Q1 2025. This simply means the managers did not do a good job in setting up the risk factors hedging correctly, and when you run 7x leverage this can hurt you.

As per its own disclosures the mReit will lose money irrespective of what Treasury rates do, and will only make money when mortgage spreads contract:

So if we look at the interest rate exposure we can see that when rates are up TWO loses value. When rates are down the mReit also loses value. The only positive scenario here is when mortgage spreads are down.

The company is set-up to produce positive net income when interest rates are not volatile (i.e. they do not move around) and when mortgage spreads are constant or contracting. In Q1 2025 that was the case, with positive net income for TWO coming from 'Net Interest and Servicing Income' at $133 mm. During that quarter the hedging was good, with the 'Mark-to-market' line item quasi-flat.

The take-away for an investor is that TWO is a volatile entity that can severely underperform due to its leverage during market turmoil. It does not care if risk free rates move up or down, because it loses money in both scenarios when markets are volatile and hedging is ineffective. TWO does best in a calm environment when it can just clip its interest and servicing income.

Baby bond

TWOD was issued in May 2025. The bond represents senior unsecured obligations for the company. Some of the characteristics and analytics for TWOD are:

  • Outstanding notional: $100 mm
  • Coupon: 9.375%
  • Par value: $25/share
  • Call date: 5/15/2027
  • Maturity date: 8/15/2030
  • Distribution dates: 2/15, 5/15, 8/15 & 11/15
  • Current yield: 9%
  • YTM: 8.4%

Since issuance the baby bonds have traded up, thus reducing the current yield and yield to maturity. Structurally speaking TWOD are a better risk/reward than the preferred shares, but like any financial product there is a price which is attractive for entry and a level which is stretched.

We find the current price stretched, especially in light of the limited upside here. While lower interest rates will help TWOD in time, a risk-off event can see the price move lower very fast, especially in light of the fundamental risk factors for the holding entity TWO which does very poorly when rates are volatile. We would love to buy TWOD at par or below, but see no upside at the current levels.

Retail investors, even buy and hold ones, need to understand that baby bonds do come down in price, and the best example that we have is the 2028 Carlyle Credit Income Fund NT 28 (NYSE:CCIA) which came down in price very fast from overstretched conditions at $26.6 per share:

You can read our 'Sell' article for that name here. The takeaway here is that an investor should not blindly buy a baby bond irrespective of the price, but wait for a good entry point. TWO is a volatile and leveraged entity that will spill over into a lower price for TWOD during times of turmoil. As we have seen with CCIA, overstretched prices do come down, and they can move lower quite fast.

We like TWOD structurally, but are of the opinion that the price is currently stretched to the upside.

Conclusion

TWOD is a bond that was issued in May 2025 and has traded up in price to $26/share with a current yield of 9%. TWO just reported its Q2 2025 financials that saw a massive hit to book value from a legacy lawsuit and a poor hedging performance during Q2 2025. While it is not time to panic, the results show how volatile this entity is, and cash leaving the company is bad for bondholders. Retail investors should not blindly buy a baby bond, but wait for a good entry point. We think the price action is currently stretched and would 'Sell' this name until a return to par.

data.title: Owlet: Back From Purgatory, Market Underappreciates Turnaround
data.description:
Owlet, Inc. (NYSE:OWLT) came up on my screen for undercovered stocks that are showing growth. I actually first heard about the company years ago, as I had a nephew who was in the NICU. When he was released, the hospital provided my brother with a monitor to track the vitals of his baby boy. It was a clunky terrible machine, and at that time he went and purchased the Owlet Sock (it was prior to FDA approval) since he found it much more effective. It has a strong customer base of testimonials and brand.

Owlet is an interesting stock that is coming back from FDA purgatory. The company was showing signs of growth until it got smacked down by the FDA. Its product was pulled from the market and the company was stuck in limbo until it could receive FDA approval. The product is back on the market and is showing signs of growth again.

While the company is rebounding and seeing strong growth, it is still operating at a loss. With its products back on the market and revenues growing the stock has the potential to climb. The stock trades at a cheap price to sales ratio of 1.26. Has the market underestimated the return of Owlet? Or is it going to continue to lose money and underperform?

Company Overview

Owlet is a digital health company focused on delivering infant monitoring solutions that provide real-time health insights to parents. The company's flagship products-Dream Sock, Dream Duo, and the accompanying Owlet Dream App-track infant vital signs such as heart rate, oxygen levels, and sleep patterns. It transmits information, including warnings if data moves into "unsafe" levels. It is quite a slick product. The sock fits over the infant's foot and ankle, just like a sock, and embedded is a monitor for tracking vital signs. These products aim to provide peace of mind to new parents while helping detect early warning signs of potential issues.

Revenue is primarily derived from product sales, with recurring engagement through the app ecosystem. The company offers sock monitoring products (Dream Sock, BabySat), Owlet Cam, Owlet 360, and Accessories. The company reports all revenues in one segment. It does not break out revenues for products vs Owlet 360. Nor is there insight into margins on the different revenue sources. The company sells through medical distribution channels, direct to consumer, retail partnerships, and through healthcare distribution (BabySat product).

FDA Shutdown

The biggest thing that affected Owlet is that on Oct 1, 2021 the company received a warning letter from the FDA. Following this letter the company halted sales of its product.

The company issued the following statement:

"Owlet received a Warning Letter from the FDA regarding the Smart Sock's regulatory status in the United States," the statement reads. "The letter we received from the agency did not identify any safety concerns about the Smart Sock; rather, the FDA asserts that the Smart Sock should be classified as a medical device in the U.S. because of the heart rate and oxygen notifications. Based on the FDA's recent letter, Owlet plans to pursue marketing authorization from the FDA for these features."

The company relied on the Smart Sock for the majority of revenue prior to the FDA warning letter. That obviously all came to a stop. At the very end of 2022 the company released the Dream Sock. The Dream Sock was not used as a medical device as certain features were left off. The company was able to sell the product as basically a high tech baby monitor. This still provided some revenue for the company.

Sales reached a high of $75.8 million in 2021. This probably would have been higher if not for the end of year FDA news. Sales were boosted by the new Dream Sock, but still fell to $69.2 million. As the issue persisted revenue continued to fall in 2023 dropping to $54 million.

FDA Approval

The FDA taketh away and giveth. The company worked closely with the FDA on the Dream Sock and submitted a de novo classification request in December 2022 for the Dream Sock's heart rate and oxygen displays. Also in October 2022, the company submitted for a prescription-only medical device to be used in home for babies under the supervision of a physician, this was BabySat.

On June 20, 2023 the company received approval from the FDA of BabySat, Owlets first medical pulse-oximetry device. The product is only available through prescription. BabySat uses pulse oximetry technology to provide a real-time display of their baby's pulse rate and oxygen saturation level (SpO2) and alerts parents when these readings fall outside of prescribed ranges. With access to this information and under the supervision of a physician, caregivers are enabled to feel confident in providing at-home care for their families-helping to reduce the strain on our valued medical resources.

This is the first foray by the company into the healthcare distribution network. I think this is a great market for the company. As a medical device prescribed by a doctor it will be covered by insurance. This opens up a new market for Owlet.

The good news continued in 2023 as the Dream Sock received FDA approval on November 9, 2023. "The FDA-cleared Dream Sock will monitor and display Baby's Live Health Readings, including pulse rate and oxygen saturation level, and will provide Health Notifications, which will alert caregivers with lights and alarm sounds if their infant's readings fall outside of preset ranges. Owlet plans to make these new medical-grade features available to all existing and new Dream Sock users upon launch, soon to be announced. These cleared features are for use with healthy infants between 1-18 months and 6 lbs to 30 lbs."

The FDA approval not only opens up the market for the company to bring its product with all its features, but also establishes a point of legitimacy for buyers. For those who previously were skeptical of the product now have some reassurance that it is an FDA approved product. They are the only company with an over-the-counter FDA approved medical pulse oximetry solution in the market. I think this will allow the company to increase sales and market share.

Following the approval sales quickly bounced back and reached a high of $78 million in 2024.

Financials

Following the FDA approval late in 2023 the company saw sales climb to new highs in 2024 with sales reaching $78 million. This growth continued in Q1 2025 with sales climbing 43% year over year to reach $21.1 million. Gross margins also improved to reach 53.7%, the gross margin increased 930 basis points year over year. The company is growing revenues and also increasing profitability. Owlet broke even on an EBITDA basis, but did still have an operating loss in Q1 of $2.7 million. Part of this beat in revenue was due to Owlet 360 the company's new subscription app, which added 50,000 users rather quickly. We will discuss that more later.

The company also raised its guidance for the year, increasing revenue estimates to $91-$95 million. On the low end that would be good for growth of 16.6% on the low end and 21.7% on the high end. The company is estimating a slower growth rate for the remaining 3 quarters of the year. A big part of that is the year over year comparison. Q1 2024 was the first full quarter with the FDA approved Dream Sock on the market. Sales for that quarter were lower at $14 million as compared to the next three quarters in 2024, each of which were over $20 million. The year over year comp is a much higher hurdle. That being said I think even the revised estimate is conservative and expect the company to beat that number. The company also noted in the conference call that it expects to be EBITDA profitable for the year.

Analyst estimates also expect continued growth with revenue continuing to grow 22.5% and 18% in 2026 and 2027.

Some things that I do not like as much from the financials:

SG&A continues to be elevated as a percentage of revenue. In 2025 SG&A made up 61% of revenues. That is not a sustainable number if the company ever wants to be profitable. I get it that the company was coming off a down year in 2023, so their costs might be elevated as a return to growth. The trend did improve to 52% of revenues in Q1 2025. Still a lot higher than it needs to be. If the company were to hold SG&A constant from last year and hit its target of $91 in revenue for the year then SG&A would hold pretty constant at 52% of revenues. So the short of it is that the company needs to cut SG&A or grow its revenues faster to reduce the hit.

The company expects the gross margin to decrease going forward for the year due to the tariff impacts. The gross margin is expected to fall to 46-50% for the year. That is a decent hit to the gross margin and means the company needs to be more efficient in other areas to get to profitability. The company manufactures in Thailand and Vietnam. There is a 10% increase on the tariff to these countries, hence the increased impact going forward. That being said, they do not have exposure to China which many competitors do. The rates there are much higher, so this could end up being a net positive for the company on pricing.

Catalysts

I think the company has the ability to drive revenue growth through a few different channels. Particularly through the Owlet 360 subscription, international sales, and healthcare partnerships (BabySat product).

The first is the initial success of Owlet 360. This is an upgrade subscription option within the app. The company released Owlet 360 on January 28, 2025. So it is a new product this year. The company only has half of Q1 with the subscription as well. I think this can be a revenue driver for the company going forward. It is an evolution from a hardware company to a full-service provider. The subscription creates recurring revenue to partner with hardware sales. As long as the company can provide sufficient value to the customer to justify paying for the subscription then this could be a long-term revenue driver. While new the company has had positive feedback. The company does not provide revenue numbers from the app, but the company noted on the conference call that they had quick uptake with over 48,000 paying subscribers. The initial cost was $5.99 for the subscription, but the company noted on the conference call that it will be increased to $9.99 a month. Even if the company does not add any more subscribers, which they will, then that is monthly revenue of $500k. I would not be surprised to see this number reach closer to $1 million in recurring revenue by the end of the year. Solid recurring revenue that should be higher margin as well. I think this is the biggest driver of future revenue and value for the company.

International sales are just getting started for the company. Owlet grew international revenues by 104% year over year in Q1. Granted this was off a small base, but regardless the company is just getting started internationally. I think the growth will continue at a high rate as the company integrates into those markets.

I think the last growth driver is the partnerships with healthcare facilities. The company has a prescription only product, the BabySat which is a great solution for the market. I had a niece who had RSV, and she had a monitor after being discharged from the hospital to do just what the Owlet does. Only it was a large piece of equipment that was inconvenient and frankly did not work well at all. I think there is a lot of room for Owlet to get some market share in that space. The company also announced a new strategic partnership with Children's Hospital of the King's Daughter of Virginia. This makes it possible for babies to leave the hospital with an Owlet. The first such partnership. These partnerships are not quick wins. Healthcare is not a market that makes quick changes. I expect more of these that can drive revenue from the BabySat product.

Risks and Concerns

The company has a customer concentration risk. The largest customer made up 37% of sales in Q1 with the next two largest customers making up 14% and 6%. That is nearly 60% of revenue from 3 customers. This is a little concerning, especially since the company does direct to consumer sales online. I would have thought that revenue would be much more spread across the many customers that purchase online. There is the risk of one of those customers pulling back and materially hurting the results of Owlet. The other concern is that the company is not garnering the DTC sales or resonating with the individual parents like it needs to.

Dilution risk is another big item for the company. The company entered into a few different warrant agreements to raise money. This was needed at the time due to the financial position of the company. Unfortunately this brings out dilution risk going forward. Much of the growth will be eaten up as the warrants are exercised, limiting stock price growth.

Then there is always regulatory risk. As the company already experienced, a single letter can shut sales down. I think this is less of a concern now that the company has FDA approval, at least less risk of a complete pull of the product from the market. They still have the risk, but it would most likely not shut sales down entirely, unless something seriously faulty were to occur with the product.

Valuation

The company is bouncing back from being sent into product purgatory. It seems that the market is not completely bought into the return. The stock trades at $8.24 a share at time of writing. It has a Price to Sales ratio of 1.26 while growing revenues at 40% for the quarter and an expected 20% for the year. This is a really low valuation based upon sales. Since the company is not earnings positive it is hard to compare profitability metrics.

The company is not profitable at the moment. This can obviously drive some concern, but it is EBITDA break even. Earnings are expected to increase drastically as the company scales. I noted the high SG&A costs. This issue will be diminished through growth and scale driving profitability. The estimates think it will be. EPS growth is expected to be 65%, 70%, and 82% for the next three years.

The cash burn for the quarter was $5.9 million. This is expected to decrease in the coming quarters. The company has over $16 million in cash on the books. So it has time to continue to grow sales and become cash flow positive.

Prior to the FDA notice the company was trading close to $140 a share. Look at the 5 year chart.

Pretty easy to see the effect of the FDA warning letter and subsequent product removal. I think the stock was well ahead of itself at that time, but it shows the potential that investors saw in the technology at one point. The stock has seen a decent trend line recently, increasing 79% over the last 6 months. Even with that increase I think the stock is cheaply priced.

Conclusion

Owlet was a growing tech and hardware company. It was sent to product purgatory by the FDA. It has since received the blessing of the FDA and began to see growth back to where it was before the dropoff. The market has not seemed to fully realize this happening. The stock is small cap and does not get as much coverage.

I think there are multiple growth drivers for the company to continue its growth trajectory. The FDA approval provides validity in the market, the subscription app is a newer recurring revenue source with room to grow, international sales are small and just getting started, and the expansion into healthcare facilities. The company needs to refine its operations and improve profitability, but I think it's heading in the right direction. I rate the stock a buy as it seems the return of Owlet is being underappreciated by the market.

data.title: Townsquare Media: What To Watch For When Q2 Earnings Are Announced
data.description:
Townsquare Media (NYSE:TSQ) is a name we have liked for a while. The Company's management team has been focused on positioning the business for the future while simultaneously managing the balance sheet to keep both creditors and shareholders happy. Townsquare has consistently paid down debt, invested in the business, and managed to return significant amounts of cash to shareholders via share repurchases and dividends which are periodically raised.

Before the market open on August 6th the Company will report Q2 results and hold a conference call. We believe there are a few key points to pay attention to, some of which may require investors to actually listen to the conference call.

Here is what we are focused on:

  1. Digital Growth
  2. Cash Flow/Cash Balances
  3. Management's Guidance For Business Moving Forward

Digital Growth

With Management guiding FY 2025 Revenues to fall within the range of $435-455 million, this leaves open the possibility of meeting or exceeding FY2024 Revenues of $451 million. It is possible to achieve this; however, with broadcast revenues declining in the high, single-digits the heavy lift falls upon the digital segment to pick up the slack - which based off of Q1 results and Management's guidance appears plausible.

While the Ignite and Interactive businesses are established and humming along, investors should also look for updates on Townsquare's latest venture, media partnerships, whereby the Company offers their digital marketing services to other broadcasters located in markets outside of Townsquare's own broadcasting markets. This will be a growth area for the Company moving forward and should reach the equivalent of roughly 10% of current annual revenues ($50 million annually in 3-5 years) for Townsquare in the next few years. Now this is a new business and coming off of a $1 million revenue quarter in Q1 2025, but CEO Bill Wilson has discussed the robust pipeline of potential partners and the Company's estimate for $6-8 million in FY 2025 revenues for this new offering. While this business is ramping up, and overall revenues in this industry ramp up from Q1 through Q4 with each quarter seeing higher revenues than the previous, we think that media partnership revenues need to come in around $2 million for the quarter if Townsquare hopes to deliver on the high-end of the estimate at $8 million. A number around $1.5 million probably means FY will be on the low end of the range simply due to being halfway through the year, still onboarding clients, and not near the run-rate to achieve the high-end. The Company had three clients before onboarding two new ones in Q2, so unless the back half of 2025 has more clients ready to onboard (potentially with multiple stations) then this quarter will be key.

So while the guidance from Management is for Q2 Revenue of $114-116 million and EBITDA of $25-26 million for Townsquare's overall revenues, the key behind the numbers is what digital is doing. Townsquare Ignite needs to have growth of 5-6% or more (with margins increasing) and Townsquare Interactive needs to be at 2% or more (with the expectation that margins slightly decrease).

Cash Flow/Cash Balances

Townsquare should generate cash flow in Q2, and for perspective in 2024 the Company generated $10.7 million in the first half of that year. The Company just refinanced its debt at the end of 2024, which impacted cash balances - and has cash at a level where we think Management is going to have to allow it to rebuild in order to continue to do significant investments, debt repayments and/or share repurchases. If doing cash flow calculations yourself, remember that adjustments are needed to factor in the fees from the debt refinancing, so the cash flow number the Company provides in their commentary may or may not need to be adjusted to compare to the $10.7 million figure from 2024 (so listen carefully!).

Cash will be impacted by Broadcast and whether margins can improve from Q1 levels (low 20s) back into the mid-20s. We know revenues will be down, but if margins can stay in the mid-20s it will alleviate the damage of a double-whammy occurring.

We would point out that Townsquare's refinanced debt is now floating rate - and while they currently pay more than they would if it were fixed-rate debt, it does provide the opportunity for the company to benefit if the US Federal Reserve leans into an easing monetary policy and ramps up rate cuts in the quarters ahead. As it stands now, Townsquare will pay roughly $9 million more in interest expense in 2025 than 2024 assuming no changes by the Fed - but debt repayment and interest rates being lowered would both lower that difference. We do not expect any commentary from the company on this specifically in their prepared comments, but the topic could be brought up in the Q&A by an analyst.

Guidance Moving Forward

Investors should pay careful attention to guidance to make sure that Management reaffirms their previously supplied guidance. With the Broadcast business under pressure and declining this year, being able to achieve guidance hinges heavily on Broadcast declining within a specific range and the digital business delivering on growth. We know from the Q1 Conference Call that tariffs impacted the business in Q2, but that the Company's customers were returning and business was picking up through the quarter. This opens up the possibility of a Q2 miss, but FY guidance being affirmed by Management - which would simply mean that ad spend was moved or delayed due to the uncertainty surrounding 'Liberation Day' back in April.

While Management is highly unlikely to give a cash-flow guidance number, we believe that as long as revenue and margin estimates a guided to a range that supports operating cash flow in the $43-45 million range it is a win for investors. That is the level we believe will allow Management to continue to be opportunistic on debt repayment and small share repurchases in 2025, while also leaving open the possibility for another small dividend hike for next year. Since bringing back a dividend in 2023, Townsquare has increased the dividend each year, and another $0.0025/share increase per quarter, or $0.01/share annual increase, would cost very little while continuing to show the market that Management is confident in the business moving forward.

Final Thoughts

We are believers that the broadcast space, both terrestrial radio and television, will become more active in the next few years as companies reposition themselves to take advantage of the FCC being more open to deals and the best operators moving to be consolidators. While we do not expect to hear any big news from Townsquare on this front, we believe investors are well served paying attention to the items we highlighted because if Management can continue to execute and position Townsquare for success, then we do believe that Townsquare could find itself in a position to handsomely reward shareholders when the next round of consolidation begins.

We remain buyers of this name, and believe that with the digital business making up a larger majority of overall revenues and profits that eventually the market will revalue the business. Until then, we are happy to collect what appears to be a "money good" dividend that yields 10%+.

data.title: Smurfit Westrock: Strong Fundamentals Despite Short-Term Challenges
data.description:
We're revisiting the Smurfit Westrock Plc (NYSE:SW) investment opportunity to provide further commentary and insights following the Q2 release. Our previous analysis explicitly stated, "The Smurfit Westrock merger presents strategic value at a discounted valuation, offering a compelling buy opportunity." We believe this company remains difficult to assess; however, it is important not to value it on a P/E basis due to its ongoing restructuring efforts.

Before the merger, we were covering both WestRock and Smurfit Kappa. Now we observe: 1) a valuation discount, 2) visible operational efficiencies with $400 million synergies, 3) the potential for peace between Russia and Ukraine that might reduce COGS, and 4) a global presence that helps mitigate risks from tariffs & counter-tariffs.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and Our Positive Take

Smurfit WestRock reported Q2 2025 results in line with expectations on an adjusted basis. Top-line sales reached $7.94 billion, and turnover missed Wall Street analysts' estimates by 0.03%. By regions, North America showed substantial improvement, recording $752 million in adjusted EBITDA and a 15.8% margin, driven by early synergy realization. The EMEA and Asian regions remained stable, while LATAM posted a standout EBITDA margin of 23.7%. Overall, the company delivered an adjusted EBITDA of $1.21 billion (15.3% margin - Fig 2). Going down to the P&L reporting, a $26 million net loss was recorded due to $280 million in restructuring and impairment costs related to ongoing facility closures. Consensus was expecting a net profit of $320.04 million.

Looking at the corrugated volumes, Smurfit WestRock reported a decline in North America and LATAM, reflecting end-market softness. The company continued investing heavily in operational upgrades, including facility closures, that are expected to deliver long-term EBITDA and CAPEX savings.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

  1. While the results may not appear entirely positive, we firmly believe that Management's reaffirmation of the full-year Adjusted EBITDA guidance of $5.0-$5.2 billion (Fig. 3) and the declaration of a $0.43 dividend are strong indicators of confidence in the company's performance. Additionally, the company has already internalized ~$180 million of the targeted $400 million in synergies, aiming for ~$350 million by year-end;
  2. During the earnings call, the management reported a minimal tariffs impact, confirming our thesis. But more importantly, the CEO confirmed that 40% of North American corrugated plants moved from loss to profit over the first semester. 60% of loss-making corrugated facilities in the region are expected to profit within a year. This essential element mentioned during the earnings call could increase margins. This strategy of value-over-volume, while optimizing costs (through mill closures), will provide earnings suitability over the long term. The CEO also noted that many European paper mills are in "desperate trouble," and anticipated closures ahead. We are not surprised to see Germany in trouble. This is an opportunity for a structure player such as Smurfit WestRock to increase its market share penetration;
  3. Despite higher interest expense, Fitch upgraded the company's long-term issuer rating to BBB+ with a stable outlook (from BBB). Furthermore, the company has already set a plan in place to lower net EBITDA leverage to 2.0x;
  4. On the upside, consumer goods companies (Pepsi, Mondelez, etc.) increasingly shift toward smaller package sizes to retain inflation-weary consumers by offering lower-priced entry points across product lines. Smaller packs often generate higher profits per unit and positively impact players such as Smurfit Westrock. While each smaller unit uses less material, the overall increase in packaging SKUs and units sold will likely drive higher aggregate demand for paper-based packaging, particularly in the F&B and personal care segments.

Fig 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

While the stock price has declined since the Q2 update and headlines have been mixed, the company delivered a decent Q2. In our initiation of coverage, we anticipated an EBITDA of $5.5 billion (above the company's yearly guidance) thanks to a tailwind from US/EU corrugated box pricing increases. We were expecting higher guidance in H1, but this was not the case, so we believe additional integration costs exist. With a $5 billion EBITDA and no change with our target multiple (8x), our enterprise value reached $40 billion. This EBITDA target multiple is at a 20% discount on the Packaging Corp of America of a 10x EV/ EBITDA multiple. This valuation also aligns with the sector median FWD EV/EBITDA average (Fig. 4). With the current net debt, Smurfit WestRock equity value reached $27.2 billion, and we derive a target price of $51.48 per share. This implied a 14% upside, without the current 3.5% dividend yield. This is a base case scenario, and we do not consider demand recovery and full commercial improvement initiatives.

Fig 4

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) lower corrugated demand, 2) inventory management from clients, 3) integration risks and combined deal synergies, 4) change in COGS (energy price, chemicals, and pulp) combined with wage inflation, 5) FX changes, and 6) higher competition even if the market is progressing with a sector consolidation.

Conclusion

Although Smurfit Westrock reported negative profit, we believe this is due to temporary factors such as restructuring costs and higher interest expenses, which the company aims to resolve over time. At this price, the company remains attractive, and considering the several moving parts post-merger, we remain overweighted.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Magnolia Oil & Gas: Another Improvement To Its Guidance As It Approaches 100,000 BOEPD
data.description:
Magnolia Oil & Gas (NYSE:MGY) reported another strong quarter of production results, with Q2 2025 production around 1% above its guidance along with D&C capex below expectations. The continued production outperformance allowed Magnolia to raise its full-year total production guidance by another 2% with no change in its full-year capex budget.

Magnolia now expects 10% year-over-year total production growth, and I believe that it can also deliver around 4% to 5% oil production growth.

Magnolia also expects to benefit from reduced cash income taxes which may add up to around $30 million to $35 million per year in savings based on current 2025 and 2026 strip prices.

I project that Magnolia can generate approximately $250 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow and have increased its estimated value from $25 per share to $26 per share (at long-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas).

With Magnolia trading at around $24 per share though, I have moved back to a neutral outlook on it. Magnolia was just slightly undervalued enough for me to consider it a buy last time I looked at it.

Q2 2025 Results

Magnolia expected approximately 97,000 BOEPD in Q2 2025 production (roughly flat compared to Q1 2025 production) with $110 million in D&C capex. It ended up producing approximately 98,200 BOEPD with around $95 million in D&C capex. Magnolia's total production and oil production both increased by approximately 2% compared to Q1 2025.

Magnolia generated $107 million in free cash flow during the quarter, of which approximately $49 million went to repurchasing 2.2 million shares of common stock. Another $29 million went towards Magnolia's $0.15 per share quarterly dividend.

Magnolia also made a number of small acquisitions in late June and early July that added 18,000 net Giddings acres and around 500 BOEPD (approximately 35% oil) in production for a combined total of $40 million. These acquisitions did not impact Magnolia's Q2 2025 production.

Magnolia's lease operating expenses dropped by 10% per BOE from Q1 2025 to Q2 2025, helped by lowered workover expenses. While Magnolia expects a partial rebound in the second half of the year, it still expects its 2H 2025 lease operating expense to be several percent lower than Q1 2025 levels.

Notes On Guidance

Magnolia revised its total production guidance upward again, calling for +10% year-over-year production growth, up roughly 2% from its prior guidance and around 4% from its original 2025 guidance.

Magnolia has maintained its full-year capex budget at around $430 million to $470 million (which is around $25 million lower than its original guidance). The recent small acquisitions would add only around 0.3% to Magnolia's full-year 2025 production, so most of its guidance improvement is from continuing strong well productivity and capital efficiency.

2H 2025 Outlook

Magnolia may now end up with around 98,700 BOEPD in full-year 2025 production, including around 40,000 barrels per day of oil production.

This would mean that Magnolia would average around 100,000 BOEPD in 2H 2025 production, with around 40,500 barrels of oil production per day. This is roughly 2% production growth compared to Q2 2025 levels.

The current strip for the second half of 2025 is roughly $68 WTI oil and $3.40 Henry Hub natural gas now.

At those commodity prices, Magnolia may be able to generate around $689 million in 2H 2025 revenues.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 7,452,000 $66.00 $492
NGLs 5,152,000 $21.00 $108
Gas 34,776,000 $2.55 $89
Total Revenues $689

Magnolia expects its lease operating expense to end up around $5.25 per BOE during 2H 2025. It also expects minimal cash income taxes for 2025 and 2026 now (at current strip) with the impact of the new tax legislation. It paid $8 million in cash income taxes in 1H 2025 and I had previously modeled around $35 million in cash income taxes for the full-year, so this is incrementally positive for Magnolia.

$ Million
Lease Operating $97
Gathering, Transportation, and Processing $34
Taxes Other Than Income $40
Cash G&A $34
Net Cash Interest $8
Capex $225
Cash Income Taxes $0
Total $438

Magnolia is now projected to generate $251 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow, which would give it around $194 million to put towards share repurchases and minor acquisitions after paying its current $0.15 per share quarterly dividend.

Notes On Valuation

I have slightly increased my estimate of Magnolia's value from $25 per share to $26 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas.

This increase is partly due to Magnolia's continued production outperformance, with its production guidance improving by a couple percent. I have also assumed that Magnolia's oil production ends up around 4% to 5% higher year-over-year (slightly above its original low-single digit guidance for oil production growth).

As well, Magnolia's cash income taxes might go from around $35 million per year (in 2025 and 2026) at the current strip to a negligible amount, so that in itself would add around 18 cents per share to Magnolia's annual free cash flow in the near term.

Conclusion

Magnolia continued to report strong production results, leading to another increase in its 2025 production guidance without increasing its capex budget. Magnolia is also benefiting from lower cash income tax expectations and may be able to generate around $250 million in 2H 2025 free cash flow at current strip prices.

Magnolia's capital efficiency and well outperformance (along with the income tax savings) results in its estimated value increasing from $25 per share to $26 per share. It is trading around 8% below that level, so I'd consider it marginally undervalued, but not quite enough to consider it a buy at its current price.

data.title: FDV: Active Dividend ETF With Performance Restrained By Low Beta
data.description:
Federated Hermes U.S. Strategic Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:FDV), an actively managed dividend-focused ETF leveraging the potential of the quality and low volatility factors, attracted my attention just about two and a half years ago.

And while I dedicated quite a few paragraphs of the first article to the discussion of why FDV is a decent product, I was not open to the idea of starting coverage with a Buy rating, as a too-short trading history always holds me back from being confidently bullish on an ETF.

However, when I was revisiting it in April of this year, this logic was no longer working, as its trading history had increased to about two years and five months, which was already long enough to prepare some relevant annualized data. Moreover, the most recent performance back then was speaking in favor of it, as the tariff uncertainty was reverberating through the markets, but FDV was not in the camp of those most afflicted, as its low beta exposure combined with its high quality secured some immunity to the chaos. However, I still warned that the exact same low beta exposure could detract more than contribute, and maintaining a large allocation to it means quite significant gains missed.

And this time, I am doing the exact same thing, with the main difference being that my point on low beta is now reinforced with more evidence: since the previous coverage, FDV has grossly underperformed the S&P 500, which it is benchmarked against.

So before we proceed to the discussion, which, as usual, will include a deep dive into performance and factor nuances, I should clarify that I maintain the Hold rating, which mostly reflects its strong quality exposure. However, I am not in the bullish cohort, and there is a diminutive chance that I will become bullish on FDV in the future. I see no reason why quality- and low volatility-oriented investors should favor it over the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD), which has a lower expense ratio, a higher dividend yield, and is more liquid. I will provide data to corroborate in the ensuing paragraphs.

What Is FDV's Strategy?

According to the Federated Hermes website, the ETF is managed actively, with the goal being twofold: income and capital appreciation, and "investing primarily in high dividend-paying U.S. stocks with dividend growth potential" is what is supposed to allow FDV to achieve these goals. But what stocks fall into that category? As described in the prospectus:

The Adviser defines high-dividend-paying stocks as those with a higher dividend yield than the S&P 500 Index’s average dividend yield.

Overall, the methodology is fundamentals-oriented:

The Adviser’s security selection process involves prioritizing stocks based on attractive combinations of dividend yield and dividend growth potential over time following review of appropriate fundamental criteria, including, but not limited to, balance sheet strength, earnings growth, and cash flow durability. Those companies that rank as highly attractive in the prioritization process are closely scrutinized for inclusion in the portfolio using bottom-up fundamental proprietary research.

FDV Performance: Disadvantages Of Low Beta Are Crystal Clear

Despite being an actively managed ETF, FDV is quite reluctant when it comes to adding and removing stocks. For instance, since my April article, Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) was ousted and Sysco Corporation (SYY) was added, and that's it. And to my pleasure, it makes returns attribution a much easier task compared to high-turnover ETFs.

So why has FDV underperformed the S&P 500 amid such buoyant conditions? My calculations show that 13 stocks that were in the red are the main detractors, chief among them being Clorox (CLX), which has declined by around 9.7% between April 18 and July 30. Overall, the consumer staples sector was one of the worst performing over this period, with the average price return of -2.29%. Utilities, FDV's favorite sector, were also performing sluggishly for the exact same reason as consumer staples: the defensive thesis lost its allure as the markets returned to greed. Financials lent some support but have not changed anything fundamentally.

Sector Weight in FDV as of July 30 Average price return
Communication 3.5% -3.9%
Consumer Staples 12.5% -2.3%
Materials 4.6% 0.3%
Industrials 5.4% 2.3%
Utilities 18.0% 2.7%
Real Estate 8.1% 2.8%
Consumer Discretionary 3.3% 7.8%
Health Care 15.1% 8.3%
Energy 9.2% 13.1%
Information Technology 4.4% 18.5%
Financials 14.3% 27.6%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF. Based on the share prices as of April 18 and July 30. Data for SYY and BMY was not included

And as I promised, below is the data to prove that low beta was the culprit.

Group Median 24M Beta
Stocks that were down over the period 0.24
Stocks that were up over the period 0.62
Stocks that rose by over 20% over the period (9 in total) 1.14

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF

However, I do not want my analysis to look one-sided, so a few words should be said in defense of FDV. The fact is, its YTD total return is quite close to that of IVV thanks to its January–March momentum, while SCHD is barely in the green chiefly because of the energy sector's volatility that has detracted a lot from its returns, with consumer staples (especially names like PepsiCo (PEP) and Target (TGT)) being the second main detractor.

Sector Weight in FDV Weight in SCHD
Utilities 18.02% 0.04%
Health Care 15.12% 15.79%
Financials 14.30% 9.08%
Consumer Staples 13.51% 18.84%
Energy 9.15% 20.02%
Real Estate 8.13% 0.00%
Industrials 5.38% 11.83%
Materials 4.58% 3.16%
Information Technology 4.39% 8.34%
Communication Services 3.53% 4.39%
Consumer Discretionary 3.32% 8.19%

Created by the author using data from the ETFs. Weights as of July 30

The SPDR® Portfolio S&P 500® High Dividend ETF (SPYD), which has methodological similarities with FDV, also performed much weaker, while the Franklin U.S. Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (LVHD) beat it slightly.

Also, I believe it is necessary to revisit its long-term performance, even though since April, with three full calendar months added, not much has changed.

We see that over December 2022–June 2025, FDV was incapable of at least matching the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF's (IVV) return, let alone exceeding it. The good news is that passively managed peers SCHD and LVHD did much worse.

FDV Portfolio: SCHD Is Still Mostly Ahead

My approach today is similar to one I opted for in April: juxtaposing FDV's factor story to those of SCHD and IVV. For clarity, IVV fits excellently in this comparison, as it tracks FDV's benchmark. And SCHD is selected mainly because it is one of the largest and most liquid dividend ETFs in the U.S. with an immense following (and an army of skeptics, too).

A necessary remark is that in the table above, EV/EBITDA was adjusted for IVV to eliminate the effect of figures over 100x on the final result.

So as of July 30, there were 51 equities in the FDV portfolio, most of them being value stocks from the S&P 500. Overall, we see a relatively high-dividend, high-quality, growth-light mix, with little change over the past few months. I like its value tilt that is in part illustrated by the portfolio's weighted average dividend yield of 3.75% (the dividend yield of the ETF itself is 2.94%), 3.2x that of IVV. But still, there is nothing particularly impressive here:

  • SCHD is mostly cheaper (its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher confirms that).
  • SCHD's holdings are of higher quality (Return on Assets, adjusted Return on Equity, and Return on Total Capital, etc., tell a lot).
  • Factor in SCHD's 6 bps expense ratio vs. FDV's 50 bps and the superior liquidity of the former, and a case for FDV becomes even more complicated to defend.

Final Thoughts

In sum, FDV is a low-beta dividend ETF that is doing what it is supposed to by design: delivering the yield that is well in excess of IVV's, performing a little better than the market during downturns, and then, inevitably, lagging it during recoveries.

So my conclusion today is that FDV has a few issues, but they are not serious enough to cross it off the dividend ETF watchlist and forget. This fund does deserve at least some investor attention. However, a Buy rating is excessive and unjustified.
